summary: Value in organizations is hierarchical and transitive, flowing from shareholders down through executives, management, and individual contributors. During layoffs, data professionals must recognize that shareholder priorities ultimately determine what counts as valuable, and align their work accordingly. The challenge is that many data professionals have responsibility without authority, creating a frustrating gap between producing insights and delivering recognized value.
key_points: Value is subjective, dynamic, and determined by whoever holds power - ultimately shareholders and boards of directors
Value flows transitively through organizational hierarchy: Shareholders > Executives > Management > Individual contributors
Data professionals often have responsibility without authority, leading to frustration when insights can't translate to action
What shareholders value changes - tech companies shifted from prioritizing growth to demanding cash flow and profitability
To add value, understand your company's highest-order mission and ensure your work aligns with what those who control your destiny find valuable
money_quotes: The recent layoffs are a reminder of the Golden Rule - whoever holds the gold makes the rules.
It's common to see data professionals granted a lot of responsibility but little to no power to take action on the insights from the data they produce.
Not all value is created equal. Value is transitive.
You and your team need to be in violent agreement with the business about what value means, how it is measured, and who is responsible for delivering it.
Your true north is knowing your ultimate customer and what they value.
key_insight: Data professionals must recognize that value is ultimately defined by shareholders, not their immediate stakeholders, and must align their work with the organization's highest-order priorities while advocating for the authority needed to act on their insights.
